title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for the Military Road project and consulting services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Public Comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 Meeting Minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permit
CIP Update
Street Operation Update
Utility Operating Update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Resolution 2026-014 Transportation Funding
Recycled Asphalt Base Course Production
Sanitary Sewer Discharge Violation Discussion
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
Military Road Construction Agreement
Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the approximately $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for settlement issues over sanitary sewer trenches, and upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street by contractors.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant flooding required dewatering operations near Weston Avenue for approximately one week. Discussion addressed potential permanent pump solutions for recurring flooding issues in the Crane Meadows area. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of an additional 18-inch culvert.

Josh reported on water service repairs, lift station maintenance, and ongoing clear water infiltration issues. April wastewater flows showed 53-54 million gallons treated versus only 37 million gallons of water pumped into the system, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges directly into sanitary sewers as a significant contributor. Kennedy Park Farmers Market received positive feedback with no parking or traffic issues reported.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total, with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions at the state level. Current state funding doesn't adequately cover recurring operational costs like street maintenance and snow plowing, requiring general tax subsidies. Motion to adopt Resolution 2026-014 passed unanimously.

Michael presented the recycled base course crushing contract, noting King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons ($126,900 total). Analysis showed the village's recycling program saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 in savings across this year's three projects. Motion to award contract to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly for residents discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers. The committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100/day for violations. An inpipe technology study will help identify problem areas. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex developer at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs temporary water/sewer connection via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction extends mains through Outlot 1. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The village's share for water and sewer work on the Rothschild-led Military Road project is $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Water main relocation is needed due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion to recommend approval of the agreement passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 and Clark Dietz proposed $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than interns. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 village share
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelnik project ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel project ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel project
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact on treatment costs

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with conditions.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items
Adjournment

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline running under the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers (up from current 40) for future growth. An alternate storage shed for evidence overflow was discussed, with commissioners suggesting it be placed further north to allow for future building expansion. The motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commissioners agreed it should be contacted about compliance. The motion to approve the program passed unanimously, with an amendment to report back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites and emails to commissioners.

Electric vehicle charging stations were noted for a future agenda, likely in the fall. Discussion arose about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on TIF qualifications that could be shared with commissioners or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing the Green Heck Turner building as an example of excessive requirements. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting on actual site visits and findings.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for Board of Trustees approval with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation in wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee annually
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg regarding wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites for comprehensive plan steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be discussed at future meeting in fall

title: Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Veterans Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes and received overviews of department missions, budgets, and key initiatives including homelessness response, veteran suicide prevention, and meals on wheels programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute (no items this meeting)
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties and responsibilities
Health Department overview presentation
Health Department budget discussion
Social Services Department overview
Veterans Service Office overview presentation
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion carried with all in favor, none opposed.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administrative teams. She highlighted public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates (two-year-olds), and a new FTE position for homeless system response created through a task force last session. Administrator Leonard noted approximately half of the department's $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with the rest from public charges for services (environmental health inspections) and intergovernmental revenues (grants).

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa (not present). The department has approximately 140 FTE with a 2026 budget of over $22 million, but only about $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting many participants had never had credit accounts, bank accounts, or formal leases. He mentioned potential requests for restructuring within units due to increased need, including possibly converting a lead worker position to supervisory in child support.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo presented on the 3.0 FTE office serving approximately 8,149 interactions in 2025 (up from 5,900). He reported veterans received $44 million annually in compensation and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), and surviving spouse benefits outreach. The Man of Honor Society provided $116,000 to local veterans in 2025. When Supervisor Kremes asked about adequate staffing, Galindo acknowledged 'no, we don't have enough time' but stated they're doing well with current resources.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) serving as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. He highlighted significant financial challenges with nutrition program funding remaining flat at 2024 levels while food costs increased 40-45% over four years. The ADRC had not requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. The organization has about 75 staff and 385-400 volunteers critical to the meals on wheels program. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: Minutes from April 1st meeting approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan information to upcoming meetings
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)
Committee members invited to submit agenda items via email to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard

title: Marathon County Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously passed a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for severe weather debris following the recent tornado and flooding that devastated the Ringle area. The board also approved consent agenda items and budget amendments for social services and out-of-home placement reserves.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice and roll call
Committee appointments presentation and approval
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out-of-home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Announcements and requests
Adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing supervisor leadership, and honoring supervisor preferences. Gibbs noted that 34% of the board is in first or second terms and most supervisors received their first or second choice. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elders. Paper ballots were distributed for the vote. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee at-large positions.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
discussions.body: County Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for severe weather debris. The state waived $13.10/ton, Ringle waived $2.33/ton, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94/ton (commercial rate minus state and local waivers) to cover costs. Leonard noted the county won't meet the $670,743 damage threshold for state disaster aid but expects Ringle will. Public comment was allowed by special dispensation for Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, who requested Marathon County waive fees entirely like other entities. Multiple supervisors discussed concerns about timing, insurance delays, and future policy. The board suspended rules unanimously to bring the resolution forward, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

Resolution 24-26 was presented from Health and Human Services and HR Finance and Property Committees. Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Resolution 25-26 to amend the 2026 social services budget was presented. Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty urged the county to waive fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees, and that local businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts. She requested a six-month waiver period. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents with demolished homes, requesting fee waivers for underinsured homeowners and noting many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived at the 44 affected homes.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out-of-home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for certified severe weather debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months

title: Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver proposal to the county board that would only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31st, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone land from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the town of Green Valley on behalf of James Offer and David Tract. The town board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation; staff explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make structures conforming uses rather than non-conforming. The town board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Miskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Significant debate ensued with Supervisor Drew questioning where to draw the line for future incidents. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness compared to individual tornado victims elsewhere. County Administrator Lance Pliml explained this was triggered by the governor's emergency declaration. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the proposal to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor K and passed. The amended resolution then passed to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented a comprehensive overview of the solid waste department, covering landfill operations, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges, and upcoming capital projects. He noted the department serves as a regional facility accepting waste from Portage and Shawano counties and highlighted the $6 million cell construction project starting this year.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff, 17 county parks, and 30,700 acres of county forest. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest's economic impact of $3.25 million from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced that Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract property forwarded to county board for approval
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property forwarded to county board for approval
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance regulations, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized razing a condemned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request from Mount Olive Church, deferring decisions on both items pending additional information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
E-bike ordinance amendments in Chapter 82
Overnight parking regulations discussion
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50 regarding nuisances, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet specifications were switched as requested). Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. The motion passed unanimously.

The committee extensively discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance, with particular focus on emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, and trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns. Committee members debated whether to exempt children's electric scooters, with Chief Hunt suggesting language like 'with exemption to kids play scooters' to allow officer discretion. Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval with the addition of prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and any public property in the Village of Weston. Seconded by Sherwanka, the motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4 AM to 4 PM, October 31 to May 1) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Public Works Director Michael Wodalski noted the village could identify 6-8 problem areas and sign those specifically rather than village-wide restrictions. Chief Hunt advocated keeping overnight parking restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing begins. The committee decided to defer the item until July, with Wodalski tasked to identify specific problem areas requiring signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their facility and DC Everest High School parking lot where parishioners park on Sundays. Committee members noted there have been no reported incidents and existing crosswalks nearby. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-3,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-30,000. The committee agreed that if Mount Olive is willing to cover installation costs, they would recommend approval. Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering costs, seconded by Sherwanka. The motion passed unanimously. Public Works Director Wodalski will contact Mount Olive regarding cost-sharing.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendors Fire Inspection Ordinance
discussions.body: Fire Chief explained a new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. Food trucks inspected by any participating department receive a sticker valid for all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. The city of Wausau opted out to maintain their own inspection program. Fire Marshal Marty recently inspected a food truck that failed due to galvanized steel LP piping and improperly secured propane containers. Trustee Sherwanka made a motion to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. The motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors requiring correction: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near an existing apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt also identified an issue with Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski, an eight-unit building where each apartment has a separate street address. A motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011. The fire department previously declined to use it for training burns due to unsafe conditions. Staff discovered chickens being housed inside the structure in recent years. The current owners prefer the village handle demolition with costs added to their tax roll. Trustee Olsen made a motion to authorize staff to begin the raze order, seconded by Campkey. The motion passed unanimously.

Code Enforcement Officer Audrey reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement list after sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with code enforcement during summer on zoning violations and pet licensing. Reports were acknowledged unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting the grass will be destroyed by parking vehicles on it and questioning whether this violates village code prohibiting grass parking. He noted ADA parking spaces are marked on grass rather than adjacent asphalt, making wheelchair access difficult. He questioned the origin of the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks, asked about enforcement on farmers market days, and expressed concern about the age of several code enforcement cases, stating some have been on the list for years with no visible progress on at least three cases he's personally aware of.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to Board of Trustees with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Public Works Director to identify 6-8 specific problem areas for winter parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Public Works Director to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 (mobile food vendor fire inspections) to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections for E911 compliance to Board of Trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Parks Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Parks and Recreation Committee was scheduled to meet on May 26, 2026 to receive updates on Kennedy Park and the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course, and to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center, graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail, and kayak launch signage. The meeting included several community recreation facility matters relevant to Weston residents.

agenda.item: Approval of minutes from the previous meeting: April 27, 2026
Public Comments
Written Comments
Kennedy Update
Yellowbank Disc Golf Course Update
Discussion and possible action Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Discussion and possible action of Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Discussion and possible action Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

discussions.body: Director Osterbrink was scheduled to provide an update on Kennedy Park developments. Based on previous meeting minutes, this was expected to include information on the capital campaign, grants, and ongoing renovation work.
The committee was set to receive an update on the Yellowbank Disc Golf Course. This was expected to cover current status and any developments related to the facility.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to discuss and potentially take action on holding a Dog Day event at the Aquatic Center. This type of event typically allows dogs to swim at the pool at the end of the summer season.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider how to address graffiti issues on the Eau Claire River Trail. Discussion was scheduled to potentially include response measures or prevention strategies.
The committee was set to discuss and potentially approve signage for the Yellowbanks Kayak Launch. This follows previous discussions about the launch facility and its amenities.

publicComment: Public Comments and Written Comments were both listed as agenda items.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on approval of April 27, 2026 meeting minutes
Expected to consider action on Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
Expected to consider action regarding graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
Expected to consider action on Yellowbanks Kayak Launch Signs

title: Tourism Commission
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Village of Weston Board of Trustees meeting was scheduled to address a significant increase to park and recreation impact fees through a public hearing, along with numerous license renewals, multiple ordinance amendments, and several infrastructure construction projects. The meeting also included the Board of Review being adjourned to a future date and consideration of a new police department and municipal court facility site plan.

agenda.item: Public Hearing and Consideration of Ordinance No. 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 to Amend Park and Recreation Impact Fees
Approval of April 13, 2026, Special Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes
Approval of April 21, 2026, Board of Trustees Meeting Minutes
Acknowledge Reports/Minutes from Boards, Committees, Commissions
Acknowledge Reports from Departments
Work Product Transmittals including April Building Permits, Budget Status, Code Enforcement Report, and Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Consent Agenda including Vouchers, Adult Establishment License, Weights and Measures Licenses, Cigarette/Tobacco/Vaping Licenses, Lodging License, Liquor Licenses, and Salvage License renewals
Ordinance No. 26-008: Amending Mobile Food Vendors License
Ordinance No. 26-009: Amending Standing Committees
Ordinance No. 26-011: Amending Chapter 50 Nuisances
Ordinance No. 26-012: Amending Bicycles regulations
Resolution 2026-014: Support for Long-Term Transportation Fund Solution
Amendments to Remote Meeting Attendance Policy
President's Appointments to Committees and/or Commissions
Approval of Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License for Schofield Coin & Hobby LLC
Compliance Assurance Plan (CAP) Policy
Feedback on Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
Address Corrections for E911 Compliance
Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
CliftonStrengths assessments and training for Board of Trustees
Amendments to Employee Personnel Policies regarding Clothing/Equipment Allowance and Appearance
Recycled Asphalt Base Course Production Quotes
Sanitary Sewer Discharge Violation Fees
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for 2230 Radtke Avenue
Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
Construction Services Proposals for Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project
Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility Site Plan
discussions.body: The Board was set to review and consider action on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the Village.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was expected to discuss feedback regarding the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only, with no formal action scheduled.

The Board was scheduled to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to the State's Transportation Fund. This resolution would position the Village in ongoing state-level discussions about transportation infrastructure funding.
The Board was set to consider an intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction, which would formalize cooperation between governmental entities on this roadway project.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for a property at 4104 Shorey Avenue, which would require demolition of a structure deemed unfit for habitation or use.

discussions.item: Mobile Food Vendors License Ordinance Amendment
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008, which would amend Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding mobile food vendor licensing requirements.

The Board was set to consider renewal of numerous business licenses for the 2026-2027 licensing term, including adult establishment, weights and measures, cigarette/tobacco/vaping, lodging, various liquor licenses, and salvage licenses.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing any person to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items. Additionally, a formal public comment period was scheduled as part of the public hearing on park and recreation impact fees.
actionItems: Scheduled to vote on Ordinance No. 26-010 amending park and recreation impact fees
Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-015 amending the Village fee schedule
Expected to consider approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Scheduled to vote on consent agenda including vouchers and multiple license renewals
Expected to consider four ordinance amendments regarding mobile food vendors, standing committees, nuisances, and bicycle regulations
Scheduled to vote on Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation fund solutions
Expected to consider site plan approval for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
Scheduled to consider intergovernmental agreement for Military Road construction
Expected to consider construction services proposals for Alderson/Jelinek Intersection and Bloedel Ave Reconstruction projects
Scheduled to consider raze order request for 4104 Shorey Avenue

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Board of Trustees was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026, to conduct a public hearing on proposed park and recreation impact fee increases, consider multiple ordinance amendments affecting mobile food vendors, committee structures, nuisances, and bicycle regulations, and address various infrastructure projects including the Mountain Bay Metro Police Department facility and road construction agreements.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Public Hearing on Ordinance No. 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015 to Amend Park and Recreation Impact Fees

Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. Single-family fees were set to increase from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

discussions.item: Ordinance No. 26-008 Mobile Food Vendors License
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider amendments to Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding mobile food vendor licensing requirements. The specific changes to the licensing framework were set for trustee review and potential action.
The Board was scheduled to review proposed amendments to the administrative code regarding the structure and organization of standing committees within Village government.
The Board was set to consider amendments to Chapter 50 of the municipal code governing nuisance regulations within the Village.
The Board was expected to review proposed changes to traffic and vehicle regulations specifically related to bicycle use under Article III of Chapter 82.
The Board was scheduled to consider a resolution expressing support for long-term, sustainable funding solutions to address Wisconsin's transportation fund challenges at the state level.

As unfinished business, the Board was set to continue discussion on proposed amendments to Section 1.08 of the Elected and Appointed Village Officials Handbook regarding policies for remote participation in meetings.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, representing a significant public safety infrastructure project.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback regarding the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and consider recommended follow-up actions, though this item was designated for discussion only without formal action.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for a property at 4104 Shorey Avenue, which would require demolition of an existing structure.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, involving coordination with other governmental entities on road infrastructure.

discussions.item: Construction Services Proposals for Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project
discussions.body: The Board was set to review and potentially select construction services proposals for improvements to the Alderson and Jelinek intersection.

discussions.item: Construction Services Proposals for Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider construction services proposals for the reconstruction of Bloedel Avenue, a significant road infrastructure project.

The Board was scheduled to consider renewal of multiple business licenses for the 2026-2027 term, including adult establishment, tobacco, lodging, alcohol, and salvage licenses through the consent agenda process.

publicComment: Public comment was scheduled for non-agenda items with a three-minute limit per speaker. Additional public comment opportunity was included as part of the public hearing on park and recreation impact fees.

title: Board of Trustees
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Village of Weston Board of Trustees was scheduled to meet on May 18, 2026, to consider significant changes to park and recreation impact fees following a public hearing, review numerous license renewals for the 2026-2027 term, and address multiple infrastructure projects including construction proposals for intersection and road reconstruction projects.

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fees Public Hearing
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to hold a public hearing on proposed significant increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development. The proposed changes would raise single-family fees from $300 to $761, duplex fees from $250 to $563 per unit, and multi-family fees from $250 to $495 per unit, based on the Village's 2022 Public Facilities Needs Assessment.

The Board was expected to consider a resolution expressing support for a long-term, sustainable funding solution to Wisconsin's Transportation Fund. This resolution was set to address state-level transportation infrastructure funding concerns.

discussions.item: Site Plan Review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department & Municipal Court Facility
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to review and potentially act on the site plan for a new Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. This represents a significant public safety infrastructure project for the community.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: The Board was set to discuss feedback on the Farmers Market location at Kennedy Park and recommended follow-up actions. This item was designated for discussion only with no action expected.

discussions.item: Raze Order Request for 4104 Shorey Avenue
discussions.body: The Board was expected to consider a raze order request for the property at 4104 Shorey Avenue. Raze orders typically require demolition of structures deemed unsafe or in violation of building codes.

The Board was scheduled to consider Ordinance No. 26-008 amending Chapter 18 Business regulations regarding Mobile Food Vendors License requirements. This was set to update licensing procedures for food truck and mobile vendor operations.

The Board was expected to review and act on construction services proposals for two major infrastructure projects: the Alderson and Jelinek Intersection Project and the Bloedel Ave Reconstruction Project.

discussions.item: Intergovernmental Agreement for Military Road Construction
discussions.body: The Board was scheduled to consider an intergovernmental agreement related to Military Road construction, which typically involves coordination with neighboring municipalities or county government on shared roadway projects.

The Board was set to discuss potential interest in completing CliftonStrengths assessments and training, a professional development tool designed to help identify individual strengths and improve team dynamics.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda, allowing persons to address the Board for up to three minutes regarding non-agenda items, with time extensions permitted at the Chief Presiding Officer's discretion. Additional public comment opportunity was scheduled during the Park and Recreation Impact Fees public hearing.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Approve the Minutes
Audit of the Bills
Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27 (Action Requested)

2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update (Action Requested)
Activities & Athletics Code (Action Requested)

Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)
Riverview RFP Approval (Action Requested)
Adjourn

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Wausau Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program

Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Wausau City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
